Bayern End 14 Years of Pain
at the Bernabéu

By Dev Das · BarcaFutbol · 11 visualisations · Data: Sofascore
scroll

There is a specific kind of silence that falls over the Bernabéu when it realises it is losing. Not the raucous noise of a stadium in shock — more like the held breath of 77,000 people trying to will something into existence that the game itself is refusing to provide. It fell twice on Tuesday night. First when Luis Díaz slid a low finish past Lunin at the 41st minute. Then again — barely 20 seconds into the second half — when Harry Kane, back from injury, back at the biggest stage, curled one into the bottom corner and made it two.

Bayern Munich won 2-1. Their first win at the Bernabéu since 2001. Their first victory over Real Madrid in the Champions League since 2012, ending a nine-match winless run — the longest Bayern had ever suffered against a single opponent in European competition. This was history. And it arrived not with chaos or fortune, but with a pressing structure, a dominant winger, and a goalkeeper who refused to let Madrid back into the match until the 74th minute, by which point it was already too late.

It should have been more. Díaz admitted it. Kane admitted it. Kompany was diplomatic — "any win at the Bernabéu is a good result" — but his team know they left goals behind them. The second leg is at the Allianz Arena. Bayern have won the first leg away in 12 of their previous 13 two-legged ties. The arithmetic of history is brutal.

Shot-by-shot xG flow · Madrid 2.20 xG · Bayern 2.92 xG · Hover any dot for details · Data: Sofascore / Opta
01Team Radar · Shooting Profile
Normalised 0–100 · ↓ inverted · xG · Shots on Target · Shots Off · Blocked · Dribble Success % · Big Chances Missed · Shot Accuracy %
02Team Radar · Passing Profile
Normalised 0–100 · Touches · Pass Accuracy % · Key Passes · Crosses Accurate · Long Balls Accurate · Big Chances Created

The xG flow tells the story before a word is written. Bayern’s line spikes hard at the 9th minute — Upamecano’s tap-in cleared off the line — builds through Gnabry’s one-on-one at 28’, then jumps at 41’ and again 20 seconds into the second half. Madrid’s line grows gradually through the first half, plateaus while 2-0 down, then accelerates furiously in the final 20 minutes. The trajectory of that late surge is real. It came too late.

The shooting and passing radars set the context. Bayern’s 88.6% pass accuracy, 493 completed passes, Kimmich’s 77 of 85 — control without domination. Madrid edged key passes (16 to 13), big chances created (3 to 2) and shots on target (9 to 8). Mbappé alone generated 1.27 xG. Strip him out and Madrid’s remaining players produced 0.93 xG combined. Their aggregate threat was real. Neuer saved nine shots. Vinicius rounded the keeper at 61 minutes and hit the side netting. Madrid created enough to win this match. They scored once. Bayern created less, converted twice, and kept the door shut for 74 minutes. That is what controlling the moments looks like in data.

03–04
Defending & Duels

Carreras, Upamecano, and a tale of two defenders.

03Team Radar · Defending Profile
Normalised 0–100 · ↓ inverted · Def Actions · Clearances · Blocked Shots · Interceptions · Tackles Won · Dribbled Past ↓ · Errors → Shot ↓

Madrid made 24 clearances to Bayern’s 20, won more interceptions (6 vs 5) and more tackles (13 vs 12). The defensive numbers, looked at in isolation, flatter Madrid. Madrid won 56% of their dribbles to Bayern’s 39% — they were the more dynamic carrying team. Five errors leading to shots against three — and the only error leading to a goal in the match. That error was Carreras.

Here is the tragedy of Álvaro Carreras’s night: 8 defensive actions, 6 clearances, a goal-line clearance in the 9th minute to deny Upamecano. He worked himself into the ground on a flank where Olise was operating at a different speed. Dribbled past three times. Booked. The error that led to Bayern’s opening goal. Across the pitch: Upamecano, 11 defensive actions, three interceptions, dribbled past once. He also scuffed that 9th-minute tap-in when the goal gaped. In another universe it’s 1-0 before Madrid know what’s happening.

04Team Radar · Duels Profile
Normalised 0–100 · ↓ inverted · Duel Win % · Ground Duel Win % · Aerial Duel Win % · Possession Lost ↓ · Fouls · Was Fouled

Madrid won 53.2% of duels, 57.9% in the air — never easy to push around even when being outplayed everywhere else. Vinicius won 9 of 13. Bellingham, arriving at 62 minutes, won all five of his. The physical battle went to Madrid. The match didn’t.

And then there is Tchouaméni. 45 of 46 passes completed. 7 defensive actions. Dribbled past zero times. Booked for fouling Olise — harshly, he thought, vocally — and that yellow rules him out of Munich entirely. Bayern didn’t just win at the Bernabéu. They walked away with Madrid’s most important midfielder for the second leg handed to them on a card. That is not justice or injustice. That is football.

the paradox
What the numbers don’t say

Madrid had their chances. Bayern controlled the moments.

More xG. More shots on target. More key passes. More big chances created. More duel wins. More aerial duels. On almost every counting metric, Real Madrid were the superior team on Tuesday night at the Bernabéu. And they lost 2-1 at home. The gap between the statistical picture and the final score has a simple explanation: Madrid had their chances and didn’t take them. Bayern had fewer, and took them all.

Madrid didn’t lack chances. They lacked the clinical edge to turn them into goals. Mbappé generated 1.27 xG and was stopped four times by Neuer before finally converting. Vinicius had a one-on-one, rounded the keeper, and hit the side netting. Strip Mbappé’s 1.27 xG from Madrid’s total and the remaining outfield players generated just 0.93 combined — well below Bayern’s 2.92. The xG dominance was real. The finishing was not. Madrid created enough to win this match comfortably. They scored once.

Then there is the question of sequencing. Statistics measure volume. Football is decided by timing. Bayern scored in the two most psychologically devastating moments available: the 41st minute — just before the whistle that would have given Madrid a reset — and the 46th minute, literally 20 seconds into the second half before Madrid had touched the ball. Both goals arrived in the pauses between halves, the moments when momentum transfers most brutally. By the time Madrid’s statistical superiority could reassert itself, they were chasing a two-goal deficit with 44 minutes left. Their late dominance was real. It was also exactly what Bayern had planned for.

And then there is Carreras. Madrid’s defensive numbers look reasonable in aggregate — 45 actions, fewer dribbles past than Bayern, solid clearance numbers. But one player’s catastrophic night in one corridor of the pitch collapsed the entire structure. The goal-line clearance in the 9th minute bought Madrid time. The error that led to Bayern’s opening goal gave it back. Tchouaméni booked while trying to cover the same flank. The cascade effect of one position being systematically overwhelmed by Olise doesn’t show up cleanly in any single metric. It shows up in the scoreline.

The data doesn’t show one other thing: Madrid came into this on the back of a 2-1 loss to Mallorca in La Liga. Bayern came in unbeaten in 14 across all competitions. Form and momentum are invisible in match statistics. They are very visible in how a team handles the pressure of being 2-0 down at the Bernabéu with 44 minutes to play. Madrid pushed. They created. They pulled one back. They did not find a second. Madrid had their chances. Bayern controlled the moments. That is what separates the two sides in this tie.

05–08
Scatter Analysis

The margins, mapped.

05Scatter · xG vs Shots on Target
All players with xG data · Bayern red · Madrid white · Top-right = highest threat combination

Gnabry had 0.71 xG from 69 minutes — one shot on target, saved. Upamecano accumulated 0.70 xG, including the tap-in Carreras cleared in the 9th minute. Neither converted. Vinicius had a one-on-one with Neuer at 61 minutes — rounded the keeper, hit the side netting. Mbappé: 1.27 xG, four shots on target, denied every time until the 74th. Kane’s goal was his 49th of the season, Mbappé’s his 20th in the Champions League this campaign. Two of the most clinical forwards in Europe, same pitch, both scoring — Bayern still won.

06Scatter · Touches vs Pass Accuracy %
Players with 20+ minutes · Top-right = high volume + high precision · Bayern red · Madrid white

Alexander-Arnold completed 69% of his passes — lowest of any outfield starter. That is a tactical choice, not a failure. His nine crosses, 11 long balls, and the cross that curved behind the entire Bayern backline for Mbappé’s goal are why 69% is not a damning number when the one that matters arrives perfectly. Tchouaméni completed 45 of 46. He won’t be in Munich.

07Scatter · Duel Win % vs Possession Lost
Players with 5+ duels · Top-left = efficient · Bottom-right = costly · Bayern red · Madrid white
08Scatter · Defensive Actions vs Dribbled Past
All players with defensive involvement · Top-left = elite defender · Top-right = high output, vulnerable
09–11
Player Profiles

Three players. One match. All the difference.

Joshua Kimmich — Playmaker / DM

102 Touches 91% Pass Acc. 3 Key Passes 90 mins
09Pizza · Kimmich Profile
Normalised 0–100 within match context · Blue=Control · Green=Creation · Red=Defensive · Bayern Munich · 90 mins

Kimmich was the engine no broadcast camera follows. 102 touches — highest in the match. 91% pass accuracy against a press designed to break exactly that. Three key passes. He blocked the pass that started the chain leading to Kane’s goal 20 seconds into the second half. His 17 possession losses are the price of being the spine of everything. No goals. No assists. Irreplaceable.

Michael Olise — Winger

Assist 10/15 Duels Won 5 Fouls Drawn 82 Touches 90 mins
10Pizza · Olise Profile
Normalised 0–100 within match context · Red=Carrying · Yellow=Output · Green=Creation · Blue=Control · Bayern Munich · 90 mins · Assist

Olise. Where do you start. Five fouls drawn — more than any player on the pitch — because Madrid had no clean way to stop him. Every time he got the ball in the first half, the Bernabéu tensed. He drew the foul that got Tchouaméni carded. Set Kane up with a precise square pass after a mazy run that started 30 yards from goal. Stung Lunin’s palms with a shot that had no right to be as close to the corner as it was. Nine crosses, zero accurate — the one criticism. Everything else was a different class entirely. Bayern won this tie in the first leg because of Michael Olise.

Luis Díaz — Attacker

Goal · 41′ xG 0.78 89 mins
11Pizza · Luis Díaz Profile
Normalised 0–100 within match context · Gold=Goal · Yellow=Output · Blue=Control · Green=Creation · 89 mins · Goal

Luis Díaz won 2 of 9 duels. He completed 1 of 3 dribbles. For 40 minutes he was peripheral — busy but not decisive, working channels without quite getting in behind. Then Gnabry played the through ball, perfectly weighted, and Díaz was alone in the area with the ball rolling to his favoured foot, and he did what Colombians of his type do: he finished without hesitation. Low, past Lunin, 1-0, silence. His xG was 0.78 — a genuine chance, not a poacher’s tap-in. The biggest stages are where he keeps answering his critics. He said afterwards they could have scored more. He’s right. But sometimes one is everything.

What comes next: Bayern take a one-goal lead to Munich on April 15. In 12 of their previous 13 Champions League ties in which they won the first leg away, they advanced. Real Madrid will point to Mbappé, to Vinicius, to the comeback gene that has broken better teams than this one. They are not wrong. But this Bayern team — with Olise impossible to stop, Kane back and scoring, Neuer rolling back the years, and Kimmich at the spine of everything — looks like a team that believes the semi-final is already theirs. Tuesday night at the Bernabéu felt like an announcement. Munich next Wednesday is where we find out if it holds.
